master_framework_metadata.framework_description: Continuously updated master investing framework distilled from Warren Buffett shareholder letters.

principles.principle_title: Reconstruct economic reality before trusting accounting presentation
principles.principle_statement: Reported accounting is only a starting point; analysis should reconstruct the economic reality by adjusting misleading denominators, ownership attribution, segment economics, book-value relevance, and recurring earning power.
principles.normalized_principle: Judge performance and value by economically meaningful earnings, assets, ownership interests, and capital bases rather than by accounting presentation alone.
metric_definition: segment_earnings: earnings generated by each operating or investment segment viewed separately
ownership_attributable_earnings: the portion of earnings economically attributable to the shareholder's ownership stake
minority_interest: the share of subsidiary economics that belongs to outside owners rather than the parent
reported_consolidated_earnings: aggregate earnings presented under consolidated accounting rules
book_value: accounting value of equity on the balance sheet
return_on_book_capital: earnings earned on book equity
purchase_price_to_book: purchase price relative to book value
metric_usage_context: segment_earnings: separate businesses with different economics instead of judging the enterprise from one blended total
ownership_attributable_earnings: identify what earnings truly belong to shareholders after considering partial ownership
minority_interest: adjust consolidated results so reported profit is not mistaken for wholly owned economics
reported_consolidated_earnings: treat reported totals as a starting point that may hide recurring, investment-related, or accounting-driven differences
book_value: test whether accounting capital is a useful proxy for economic value
return_on_book_capital: determine whether the company earns attractive economics on the capital represented by book
purchase_price_to_book: assess whether a discount or premium to book is justified
metric_limitations: reported_consolidated_earnings: can combine fully owned, partly owned, operating, and accounting-driven results in ways that hide economic reality
segment_earnings: may still mislead if one-time distortions, restatements, or ownership effects are not separated
ownership_attributable_earnings: requires careful attribution and can be obscured by GAAP presentation
book_value: can mislead when assets are not marked in economically meaningful ways or when returns on book are poor
purchase_price_to_book: cheapness to book alone is not attractive if underlying business quality is weak
performance_logic: A ratio is only meaningful if the denominator approximates real capital employed.
Distorted book values or unusual leverage can make unadjusted ROE or similar ratios misstate true economics.
Blended reporting can mask which businesses truly create value and which only affect accounting presentation.
Accurate capital allocation requires knowing what economics are recurring, attributable, and decision-relevant.
A discount to book matters only if the book capital supports attractive economics.
Book value is most useful when asset values and the returns earned on them are economically informative.
evaluation_questions: Are assets carried at unrealistic values?
Does reported equity fairly represent the capital employed in the business?
Would return on tangible equity or return on invested capital be more informative here?
What parts of reported earnings are actually attributable to shareholders?
Which reported numbers combine businesses with very different economics?
Does the financial presentation clarify reality or hide it?
Does book value approximate economic value here?
Is the company earning high returns on book capital?
Is a discount to book justified by poor economics, or is it a genuine bargain?
diagnostic_signals: Large accounting distortions in balance-sheet carrying values.
Unusual leverage makes equity-based ratios swing wildly.
Reported ROE looks strong but underlying capital intensity is still high.
Large differences in ownership percentages across segments
Heavy reliance on consolidated figures without segment economics
One-time accounting distortions or merger restatements affecting reported results
Insurance or asset-heavy businesses where book may matter
Large discount to book without clear explanation
Poor returns on book capital despite low valuation
practical_rules: Adjust the denominator before drawing conclusions from standard ratios.
Prefer the capital measure that best reflects economic reality, not accounting convenience.
Rebuild performance by segment, ownership stake, and economic function.
Separate fully owned, partly owned, operating, investment-related, recurring, and accounting-driven items.
Analyze what management would review internally, not only what GAAP presents externally.
Use book value as context, not as a standalone bargain signal.
Pair price-to-book analysis with return-on-capital analysis.
Ignore book value when it lacks economic meaning.
example_buffett_phrasing: standard ratios are useful only if the denominator is economically meaningful
adjust the framework when accounting figures obscure business reality
Economic reality matters more than accounting presentation
Book value can matter, but only in context

Economic reality over accounting presentation
Use book value only when economically meaningful

principles.principle_title: Measure earnings against the capital required to produce them
principles.principle_statement: Management and business performance should be judged by returns earned on the capital actually required, not by EPS, profit growth, or earnings in isolation.
principles.normalized_principle: Capital efficiency is the primary scorecard: earnings growth is valuable only when it exceeds the growth in required capital and produces attractive returns.
metric_definition: beginning_equity: shareholder equity at the start of the period
capital_employed: capital used in the business to generate earnings
necessarily_employed_capital: the capital genuinely required to operate the business
return_on_capital: earnings relative to the capital required to produce them
earnings_per_share: per-share reported earnings growth over time
incremental_return_on_retained_earnings: earnings generated by additional retained capital
equity_growth: growth in shareholder capital available to the business
return_on_equity: rate earned on the growing equity base
metric_usage_context: beginning_equity: frame annual operating performance against the capital base in place
capital_employed: compare business economics across differing asset structures
necessarily_employed_capital: strip away irrelevant accounting balances and focus on what the business truly needs
return_on_capital: judge whether economics are attractive and whether future growth will need large reinvestment
earnings_per_share: compare it with equity growth to see whether reported growth reflects better economics or just more capital
incremental_return_on_retained_earnings: use it to judge the quality of reinvestment behind earnings growth
metric_limitations: reported_earnings: can look impressive even when the business absorbs too much capital
accounting_capital_base: may understate or overstate the capital truly needed if not adjusted economically
earnings_per_share: it can look strong even when incremental returns on new capital are ordinary
return_on_equity: a stable or falling return can reveal weak economics hidden by EPS growth
performance_logic: EPS can rise mechanically when the equity base expands, so earnings growth alone can overstate improvement in business economics.
Capital efficiency reveals whether management is creating more profit per dollar employed rather than merely using more capital.
Good businesses earn strong returns on the capital they require.
Capital-intensive businesses can report profits that still represent weak economics when measured against the capital absorbed.

Economic performance depends on what the business earns on added capital, not just whether earnings per share rise; growth funded by retained capital only creates value when incremental returns remain attractive.
evaluation_questions: How much beginning equity capital was required to generate current earnings?
Did earnings growth exceed, match, or lag growth in the equity base?
Is management improving business economics or only deploying more capital?
How much capital does this business need to produce and grow earnings?
Are returns high because economics are strong, or because capital is understated by accounting?
What level of incremental capital will future growth require?
Did earnings rise because the business improved or because it had more capital?
How much additional equity was needed to produce the reported earnings growth?
Is EPS growth hiding weaker returns on incremental capital?
diagnostic_signals: EPS growth is celebrated without reference to capital employed.
Equity grows as fast as or faster than earnings.
Incremental returns on retained earnings are mediocre.
Profit figures emphasized without capital context
High capital needs for incremental growth
Accounting balance sheet that obscures economically required capital
Earnings rise in step with retained capital while return on equity is mediocre
Per-share growth is celebrated even though the capital base is expanding just as fast or faster
practical_rules: Compare EPS growth against equity growth before praising performance.
Use return on beginning equity or a similar capital-based measure as the primary scorecard.
Always relate earnings to the capital required to produce them.
Use beginning equity, capital employed, or necessarily employed capital as the denominator when appropriate.
Reject growth that requires too much incremental capital for too little return.
Compare earnings growth with equity growth.
Treat EPS growth as secondary unless incremental returns on retained earnings are also attractive.
example_buffett_phrasing: return on equity, not EPS growth alone
capital efficiency is a better indicator of managerial quality than raw earnings growth
Measure returns against the capital actually required
eps growth is a weak measure of economic performance
more capital to work with

principles.principle_title: Separate operating returns from capital gains
principles.principle_statement: Single-year performance should be judged mainly by operating returns on capital, while capital gains and losses should be separated and evaluated as part of longer-term wealth creation.
principles.normalized_principle: Separate operating performance from market-related investment gains and accounting effects before judging annual results.
metric_definition: operating_earnings: earnings generated from ongoing operations excluding capital gains or losses
capital_gains: realized or unrealized gains and losses from investments rather than operations
beginning_shareholder_equity: shareholder capital at the start of the measurement period
operating_return_on_equity: operating earnings relative to beginning shareholder equity
per_share_net_worth_growth: long-term growth in net worth on a per-share basis
operating_earnings: earnings before securities gains or losses generated by operations during the period
beginning_shareholder_equity: shareholder capital in place at the start of the year, with equity securities carried at cost for this use case
return_on_equity: operating earnings generated relative to the capital base management actually employed
book_value_per_share_growth: multi-year growth in per-share net worth
capital_gains: realized and unrealized investment gains or losses over time
economic_net_worth: owner value after incorporating operations and investment values
multi_year_compounding_rate: long-run rate at which owner value grows
metric_usage_context: operating_earnings: isolate the quality of annual business operations
capital_gains: exclude from single-year operating judgment but include in long-term wealth creation assessment
operating_return_on_equity: judge whether operations generated a satisfactory return on the capital employed
per_share_net_worth_growth: evaluate long-term compounding after combining operating returns with investment outcomes
return_on_equity: use it to isolate management's annual operating performance from security-market volatility
beginning_shareholders_equity: use it as the denominator when judging the efficiency of annual operations
capital_gains: include them when judging long-term economic results because they are part of owner outcome
economic_net_worth: use it to compare long-term value creation from operations and investments together
metric_limitations: capital_gains: can distort single-year comparisons because market movements do not necessarily reflect operating performance
operating_return_on_equity: may still require adjustments if accounting treatment changes the capital base or earnings classification
per_share_net_worth_growth: is not the right measure for judging one year's operating quality in isolation
beginning_shareholders_equity: market-value swings in securities can inflate or depress the denominator and make operating performance look falsely strong or weak
return_on_equity: it misleads when non-operating portfolio marks contaminate the capital base used for the calculation
capital_gains: they are too volatile for grading a single year of operations
book_value_per_share_growth: it can understate or overstate economic reality if market values are ignored over long horizons
performance_logic: Short-term operating quality should be judged on returns generated by operations on capital employed.
Long-term wealth creation comes from both operating returns and capital gains, so the two layers should not be conflated in single-year analysis.

A stable capital base helps separate operating skill from market noise; when the denominator is distorted by portfolio moves, the reported annual return can flatter or penalize management without reflecting real operating economics.

Over long horizons, owners experience the combined result of operations and investment value changes; excluding capital gains from long-term assessment omits an important source of real economic progress.
evaluation_questions: What was the operating return on beginning equity or capital employed?
How much of total performance came from business operations versus market-related gains?
Is management benefiting from true operating strength or favorable market movements?
What capital base best reflects the capital management actually employed?
Is the denominator distorted by market values or accounting changes?
Does the chosen return measure isolate operating performance from investment-market noise?
Over a multi-year period, what total owner outcome was created?
How much value creation came from operations versus investment gains?
Are long-term results materially different from annual operating snapshots?
diagnostic_signals: A strong reported year driven mainly by investment gains
Annual results that mix operating performance with market-related gains
Accounting changes affecting comparability
Reported returns rise when the equity base is temporarily depressed by portfolio declines
Strong operations appear weaker when a rising portfolio inflates the equity denominator
Long-term value creation is much stronger than annual operating figures alone suggest
Investment holdings compound economically even when annual operating scorecards exclude them
practical_rules: Separate operating earnings, capital gains, and accounting-driven changes before judging annual results.
Use operating returns for single-year assessment.
Use per-share net worth growth or full-cycle results for long-term wealth evaluation.
Use operating earnings over beginning equity at cost for annual operating assessment.
Separate operating performance from realized or unrealized securities gains and losses.
Use one scorecard for annual operating judgment and another for long-term owner results.
Include realized and unrealized investment gains when evaluating long-term economic performance.

beginning shareholders' equity with securities carried at cost
for long-term economic performance

principles.principle_title: Normalize results across the cycle
principles.principle_statement: Single-year or peak-cycle figures are weak evidence; durable earning power should be judged across multiple years and normalized industry conditions.
principles.normalized_principle: Evaluate businesses over a full cycle because one-year results can be distorted by temporary conditions, market gains, accounting noise, or cyclical peaks.
metric_definition: normalized_earnings: earnings power under more typical conditions across a full cycle
current_return_level: the return currently being earned under present conditions
earnings_growth: the change in earnings over time
capital_employed: the capital required to support the business
metric_usage_context: normalized_earnings: estimate durable earning power instead of capitalizing temporary peaks
current_return_level: test whether current profitability is cyclical, temporary, or repeatable
earnings_growth: determine whether growth comes from improved profitability, more capital employed, or temporary industry tailwinds
capital_employed: distinguish growth caused by better economics from growth caused by a larger capital base
metric_limitations: current_return_level: can overstate durable economics in a favorable cycle
earnings_growth: can mislead if growth is driven by temporary conditions or additional capital rather than better business quality
normalized_earnings: requires conservative judgment and should not be set from one strong year
performance_logic: Short-term figures can be distorted by cycles, temporary gains or losses, and accounting noise.
A multi-year record better captures durability, compounding ability, and true business economics.
A strong year is not proof of durable economics.
The correct question is what the business can earn under less favorable but recurring conditions across a full cycle.
evaluation_questions: Over five to ten years, has the business compounded value?
Are current results cyclical, temporary, or structural?
Does the long-run record confirm or contradict the latest snapshot?
Is the current return level cyclical, temporary, or repeatable?
What would earnings look like under less favorable industry conditions?
Is growth in earnings coming from higher profitability, more capital employed, or both?
diagnostic_signals: Heavy reliance on a single-year earnings figure or a single-date market value.
Current margins or results are unusually boosted or depressed by temporary conditions.
Recent profits materially above long-run norms
Industry conditions described as favorable or cyclical
Management highlighting recent results without normalization
practical_rules: Treat one-year figures as clues, not conclusions.
Anchor judgment in multi-year return on capital and long-term value creation.
Separate current performance from normalized performance.
Do not extrapolate peak conditions into long-term value.
Analyze full-cycle earning power before committing capital.
example_buffett_phrasing: single-year results are weak evidence
treat one-year figures as clues, not conclusions
Sustainable economics matter more than headline results

principles.principle_title: Use look-through earnings for retained owner economics

principles.normalized_principle: Reported dividends or accounting income can understate value when capable managers retain earnings at attractive returns and compound per-share intrinsic value.

principles.principle_title: Validate high returns against leverage and accounting distortion
principles.principle_statement: High reported returns are meaningful only when produced by real business economics rather than leverage, under-reserving, accounting gimmicks, or cosmetic reporting.
principles.normalized_principle: Treat high ROE as evidence of quality only after testing whether leverage, accounting choices, and cash conversion support the result.
principles.primary_analytical_question: Are high reported returns real business economics or the product of leverage and accounting distortion?
performance_logic: A high return that depends on leverage or manipulation is fragile; a high return earned honestly signals better economics, stronger management, and more durable compounding.

quality_of_earnings: the degree to which reported profits reflect durable economics rather than accounting choices
cash_conversion: the extent to which reported earnings show up in cash

metric_limitations: return_on_equity: it overstates underlying business quality when leverage is high or accounting is aggressive

evaluation_questions: Is the company earning a high return because the business is good or because leverage is high?

principles.principle_title: Favor cash earnings on modest capital employed
principles.principle_statement: Superior businesses generate high cash earnings relative to capital employed and do not require profits to be continually absorbed by receivables, inventory, or working capital.
principles.normalized_principle: Cash conversion and low working-capital absorption make reported earnings more valuable to owners.
principles.primary_analytical_question: Do reported profits convert into owner cash without heavy working-capital absorption?
performance_logic: High returns are most valuable when they arrive in cash and require little reinvestment; working-capital-heavy growth can consume capital and reduce the owner's actual yield.

working_capital_intensity: how much receivables and inventory investment is needed to support operations
free_cash_flow_conversion: how effectively accounting profits convert into free cash flow

free_cash_flow_conversion: use it to distinguish accounting profits from owner cash generation
metric_limitations: cash_earnings: reported earnings can mislead when they are absorbed by receivables, inventory, or other working-capital needs
free_cash_flow_conversion: weak conversion suggests lower owner value than accounting profits imply
evaluation_questions: How much cash does the business generate relative to tangible capital employed?
Do profits show up in cash or get trapped in working capital?
Does growth require swelling receivables and inventory?

principles.principle_title: Judge results by real after-tax purchasing power
principles.principle_statement: Nominal compounding is not enough; owner success is measured by whether spendable purchasing power rises after inflation and taxes.
principles.normalized_principle: Translate nominal returns into real after-tax owner outcomes before judging business success.
principles.primary_analytical_question: After inflation and taxes, is the owner actually gaining purchasing power?

principles.principle_title: Industry structure can overwhelm managerial effort
principles.principle_statement: Business quality depends heavily on industry structure; even capable managers may earn poor returns when structural economics are hostile and improvements are easily copied.
principles.normalized_principle: Do not confuse managerial effort with favorable economics: industry tailwinds and headwinds strongly shape long-term returns.

principles.principle_title: Apply the whole-business four-part test
principles.principle_statement: A stock should be evaluated as partial ownership of a whole business and should pass all four filters: understandable business, favorable long-term economics, honest and competent management, and attractive price.
principles.normalized_principle: A sound equity investment requires business understandability, favorable prospects, trustworthy management, and a sufficiently attractive price all at once.
metric_definition: purchase_price: the market price available for the stock
intrinsic_value: rational business value based on long-term economics
metric_usage_context: purchase_price: determine whether a good business is available on sufficiently attractive terms
intrinsic_value: test whether price provides clear attractiveness relative to business value
metric_limitations: purchase_price: cheapness alone is insufficient without business and management quality
qualitative_scoring: should not be treated as a loose scoring system where one weakness offsets another
performance_logic: Treating stocks as businesses shifts focus from price movement to economics, management, and valuation.
Whole-business thinking discourages speculation and aligns analysis with intrinsic value.
Quality without price discipline is insufficient.
Cheapness without business quality is insufficient.
The method is a conjunctive filter that seeks alignment across business, management, and valuation.
evaluation_questions: Do I understand how this company makes money?
Are long-term prospects favorable?
Is the business operated by honest and competent people?
Is the stock available at a very attractive price?
Do I really understand how the business makes money and why customers stay?
Are long-term economics favorable?
Do management's behavior and communication indicate honesty and competence?
Is the stock available at a price that provides a clear margin of attractiveness?
diagnostic_signals: Investment case relies on short-term market behavior rather than business economics.
The business is not understandable or management quality is questionable.
Price attractiveness is not assessed relative to business value.
A thesis resting on only one or two strong attributes
Management quality that is unclear or untrusted
A good business that still lacks price attractiveness
practical_rules: Apply the same acquisition criteria to public stocks that you would apply to a full-business purchase.
Refuse investments that fail on business quality or management quality even if the stock is popular.
Require all four conditions before buying.
Do not offset a weak business or weak management case with mere cheapness.
Use the test as a strict filter, not a weighted scorecard.
example_buffett_phrasing: evaluate a stock exactly as if buying the whole business
understandable, favorable long-term prospects, honest and competent people, and a very attractive price
understandable business
favorable long-term prospects
honest and competent management
attractive price

principles.principle_title: Separate price volatility from business value
principles.principle_statement: Investment decisions should be anchored in business economics and intrinsic value, not short-term quotation forecasts; lower prices can improve prospective returns when the business remains sound, especially for a continuing net buyer.
principles.normalized_principle: Short-term price declines in sound businesses are opportunities rather than threats when intrinsic value remains intact and capital can keep being deployed.
metric_definition: earning_power: the business's long-term capacity to generate economic earnings
purchase_price: the price paid relative to underlying business value
intrinsic_value: the rational long-term value of the business based on future economics
future_purchase_price: the valuation available on later purchases
capital_to_deploy: additional capital expected to be invested in the future
metric_usage_context: earning_power: anchor the thesis in long-term business value creation
purchase_price: ensure the investment starts from an attractive valuation level
intrinsic_value: compare current market quotation with expected long-term business worth
future_purchase_price: judge whether continued low prices help future compounding
capital_to_deploy: determine whether remaining a net buyer changes the desirability of slow revaluation
metric_limitations: short-term market price: can reflect sentiment rather than business economics
intrinsic_value: must be estimated from business economics rather than near-term market moves
mark_to_market_gain: fast price appreciation may look good but can reduce future buying opportunity for net purchasers
performance_logic: If intrinsic business value is intact, a lower purchase price raises expected future returns.
Focusing on business performance instead of quotations reduces emotional decision-making.
Owning businesses with improving earning power compounds value over time.
Forecasting quotations is speculative and weaker than buying durable economics at a favorable price.
A recurring buyer compounds more effectively when future capital can be invested at favorable prices.
Immediate revaluation helps current holdings but can reduce long-run opportunity if more capital remains to be deployed.
evaluation_questions: Has the business thesis changed or only the stock price?
Would I want to buy more at the lower price?
Am I reacting to quotations instead of value?
Is the underlying business likely to be worth materially more in 5–10 years?
Is the thesis based on business improvement and compounding, or on near-term re-rating?
Are you buying earning power or trying to trade sentiment?
Am I thinking like a trader or like a long-term acquirer of earning power?
Would a persistently attractive market help me compound better through continued buying?
diagnostic_signals: Selling pressure occurs without deterioration in business experience.
Analyst concern centers on price movement rather than operating economics.
An investment thesis dominated by market timing
Little discussion of underlying business economics
Dependence on near-term re-rating for returns
Ongoing capital inflows or retained earnings to invest
Preference for immediate mark-to-market gains over future buying opportunities
Long investment runway
practical_rules: Separate business deterioration from quotation volatility.
Welcome lower prices when business value remains intact and capital is available.
Base decisions on business economics, management quality, and price.
Avoid relying on short-term market forecasts.
Measure success by long-term business value growth rather than near-term quotation changes.
Consider whether you are a net buyer or net seller before wishing for quick revaluation.
Value prolonged opportunity when future capital deployment is substantial.
Think in terms of compounding, not just current marks.
example_buffett_phrasing: ignore short-term market behavior when the business remains sound
market declines in a sound business are opportunities, not threats
Focus on long-term earning power, not short-term market forecasts
Prefer markets that stay favorable to buyers

principles.principle_title: Use public markets as a source of private-market bargains
principles.principle_statement: Public markets can occasionally offer minority stakes in exceptional businesses at prices below rational private-market, replacement-cost, or takeover logic.
principles.normalized_principle: Compare quoted stock prices with private-market and replacement-cost economics to identify superior public bargains.

private_market_valuation: value implied by negotiated acquisitions of whole businesses
replacement_cost: the cost to recreate the business or asset base
book_value: accounting value of equity
intrinsic_value: rational economic value of the business
metric_usage_context: stock_market_valuation: identify when minority stakes are available cheaply
private_market_valuation: benchmark public opportunities against what full-control deals imply
replacement_cost: test whether the public price is below rational asset recreation logic
book_value: provide context when book is economically meaningful
intrinsic_value: determine whether quoted prices materially understate business worth
metric_limitations: book_value: is useful only when assets and returns on those assets have economic meaning
private_market_valuation: control prices can differ from minority prices, so the comparison must remain economically grounded
stock_market_valuation: can reflect pessimism rather than business quality
performance_logic: Quoted markets can become temporarily pessimistic even when underlying business value is intact.
Minority ownership may capture much of the economics at a lower entry price than full control.
Market pessimism can let investors buy better businesses more cheaply in public markets than they could in negotiated private transactions.
When management is already excellent, control is not required to capture the business economics.
evaluation_questions: Would this business command a much higher value in a private transaction?
Is the public market discount driven by sentiment rather than impaired economics?
Does minority ownership capture sufficient economic upside?
Am I getting superior business quality at a discount to inferior private-market transactions?

principles.principle_title: Let price enforce patience
principles.principle_statement: The limiting factor is often not finding admirable businesses but waiting until the price becomes attractive enough to act.
principles.normalized_principle: Inactivity is rational when business quality is attractive but price does not yet provide sufficient return or margin of attractiveness.
metric_definition: purchase_price: current market price available for the business
target_price: the price level at which the opportunity becomes compelling
intrinsic_value: estimated business value against which price is judged
metric_usage_context: purchase_price: determine whether action is justified now or patience is required
target_price: define what valuation would make the idea compelling
intrinsic_value: anchor patience in business value rather than emotion
metric_limitations: activity_level: a desire to act is not evidence that price is attractive
purchase_price: even excellent businesses should be rejected when price is not compelling
performance_logic: Return quality depends heavily on entry price.
Patience preserves capital until a high-quality business can be bought on sufficiently favorable terms.
evaluation_questions: Am I forcing action because I want activity?
Is the business good, but simply not attractively priced?
What price would make this opportunity compelling?

Admired businesses trading at prices that do not justify action
Weak articulation of required price discipline
practical_rules: Treat inactivity as acceptable when price is not right.
Define what price would make the investment compelling.
Let price, not boredom, trigger action.
example_buffett_phrasing: Price is often the bottleneck, not idea generation

principles.principle_title: Concentrate only when conviction and price are strong
principles.principle_statement: Concentration is rational only when understanding, management trust, business quality, and valuation all support high conviction; diversification should not hide lukewarm judgment.
principles.normalized_principle: Position size should follow genuine conviction backed by business understanding and attractive price, not activity, habit, or false diversification.
metric_definition: position_size: the share of capital committed to one investment
purchase_price: entry valuation relative to business economics
intrinsic_value: estimated business value supporting conviction
metric_usage_context: position_size: reveal whether conviction is truly high or only verbal
purchase_price: confirm that concentration is backed by attractive valuation rather than admiration alone
intrinsic_value: support sizing with a clear value gap
metric_limitations: position_size: small positions in mediocre ideas can dilute results and attention
diversification: should not be used to hide lack of conviction or understanding
performance_logic: Capital and attention earn more when focused on the best-understood and best-priced opportunities.
A portfolio of weakly held ideas often reflects insufficient conviction rather than prudent risk control.
evaluation_questions: Is this a high-conviction idea or a placeholder?
Am I buying because it is truly attractive, or because I want to stay busy?

practical_rules: Size up when understanding, management quality, and price all align strongly.
Avoid buying a little of many mediocre ideas.
Let conviction and valuation, not habit, drive concentration.

principles.principle_title: Prefer a good business at a fair price over a bargain bad business
principles.principle_statement: A good business bought at a fair price is usually superior to a poor business bought at a bargain price because weak economics and turnarounds often consume capital, time, and attention.
principles.normalized_principle: Business quality should outweigh statistical cheapness when the cheap asset has structurally poor economics.
principles.primary_analytical_question: Is this investment genuinely cheap, or is it a low-quality business masquerading as a bargain?
performance_logic: Low price cannot reliably repair poor economics; weak businesses often require repeated fixes, absorb capital, and still fail to compound well, while good businesses can justify less dramatic entry prices.
metric_definition: valuation_discount: apparent cheapness relative to price, assets, or earnings
return_on_capital: economic return produced by the business over time
owner_yield: cash benefit available to owners after reinvestment needs
restructuring_frequency: repetition of turnaround or repair efforts required to sustain the business
metric_usage_context: valuation_discount: use it only after assessing whether the business itself is structurally sound
return_on_capital: use it to judge whether cheapness compensates for weak economics
restructuring_frequency: use it as a sign that the investment thesis depends on a fragile turnaround
metric_limitations: valuation_discount: cheapness alone can mislead when the business has persistently poor economics
owner_yield: weak businesses may consume capital and time despite looking statistically inexpensive
evaluation_questions: Is the thesis based mostly on cheapness rather than business quality?
Is this a bargain or a structurally inferior business that looks optically cheap?
Would the same capital earn better results in a higher-quality business at a fairer price?
diagnostic_signals: Persistent low returns despite cheap valuation
Repeated restructuring or turnaround stories
High-quality businesses trading above asset value but compounding well
practical_rules: Prefer durable business quality over statistical cheapness.
Be skeptical of turnaround-driven theses and poor businesses sold as bargains.
example_buffett_phrasing: turnarounds seldom turn
a good business purchased at a fair price

principles.principle_statement: Excellent businesses should not be sold or traded merely to match a one-year market outlook; portfolio action should be driven by business quality, valuation, and thesis change.

principles.principle_statement: Long-term compounding is higher quality when achieved with low financial and operating leverage, limited share issuance dependence, and resilience under stress.
principles.normalized_principle: High growth and compounding are most valuable when they are internally durable rather than engineered by leverage or balance-sheet stretching.

principles.principle_title: Avoid long-duration fixed nominal claims in inflation
principles.principle_statement: Very long-term fixed-rate bonds and similar nominal claims are dangerous in inflationary environments because they lock owners into returns whose purchasing power can deteriorate for decades.
principles.normalized_principle: Long-duration fixed nominal claims are unattractive when inflation threatens real purchasing power.
principles.primary_analytical_question: Does this fixed-income exposure lock capital into poor real purchasing-power economics?
performance_logic: A fixed claim on money becomes less valuable when currency purchasing power falls; the longer the duration, the more the owner is locked into poor real economics.

real_yield: nominal bond return after inflation
inflation_expectations: expected future erosion of currency purchasing power
mark_to_market_loss: decline in bond value as rates rise or real economics worsen
metric_usage_context: duration: use it to evaluate how exposed the instrument is to inflation and fixed nominal pricing of money
real_yield: use it to test whether the bond preserves purchasing power
metric_limitations: real_yield: nominal coupons can mislead when inflation is high
duration: long time locks can create severe economic damage even when accounting treatment softens the appearance
evaluation_questions: Is the instrument fixing nominal returns too far into an inflationary future?
What happens to real purchasing power if inflation persists?
Is accounting treatment hiding economic impairment?
diagnostic_signals: Low or negative real yield
High duration in an inflationary regime
Economic impairment masked by accounting conventions
practical_rules: Be skeptical of long-duration nominal bonds in inflationary periods.
Judge fixed-income securities in real purchasing-power terms, not coupon terms alone.

fixed price on money for decades

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Operating ROE on relevant capital base
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Single-year operating performance should be judged using earnings on an economically relevant beginning capital base rather than a denominator distorted by market swings.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: A stable capital base helps separate operating skill from market noise; when the denominator is distorted by portfolio moves, the reported annual return can flatter or penalize management without reflecting real operating economics.
merge_decision_log.reason: Adds denominator discipline to the existing annual operating-performance versus capital-gains distinction.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: EPS growth is not economic performance
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Earnings growth should be judged against the incremental capital required to produce it, because EPS can rise even when capital deployment is mediocre.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: Economic performance depends on what the business earns on added capital, not just whether earnings per share rise; growth funded by retained capital only creates value when incremental returns remain attractive.
merge_decision_log.reason: Same core capital-efficiency idea: EPS growth must be judged against capital required and incremental returns.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: High ROE without leverage or gimmicks
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: High returns are meaningful only when they are produced by business quality and managerial skill rather than leverage or accounting distortion.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: A high return that depends on leverage or manipulation is fragile; a high return earned honestly signals better economics, stronger management, and more durable compounding.
merge_decision_log.reason: Distinct analytical test: high ROE must be validated against leverage, accounting distortion, and cash conversion.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Long-term performance should include investment gains
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Long-term owner results should include investment gains and economic market values rather than only annual operating earnings.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: Over long horizons, owners experience the combined result of operations and investment value changes; excluding capital gains from long-term assessment omits an important source of real economic progress.
merge_decision_log.reason: Adds the long-term counterpart: capital gains and market values belong in multi-year owner-result measurement.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Judge outcomes by after-tax purchasing power
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Business success should be judged by real after-tax purchasing power rather than nominal book-value or earnings growth.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: Nominal growth can be swallowed by inflation and taxes; only returns that exceed both burdens increase the owner's real spending power.
merge_decision_log.reason: Introduces real after-tax purchasing power as the owner-level performance standard.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Compare business returns to the owner's misery index
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: A business must earn more than inflation plus owner tax friction to preserve purchasing power.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: Owner economics depend on a hurdle rate set by inflation and taxation; when business returns fail to clear that hurdle, retained earnings can reduce real wealth even without visible accounting weakness.
merge_decision_log.reason: Adds a distinct practical hurdle-rate test under real owner returns: inflation plus tax drag.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Favor high cash earnings on capital employed
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Businesses are superior when they generate high cash earnings on capital and do not need heavy working-capital absorption to realize profits.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: High returns are most valuable when they arrive in cash and require little reinvestment; working-capital-heavy growth can consume capital and reduce the owner's actual yield.
merge_decision_log.reason: Introduces cash earnings and working-capital absorption as a separate owner-earnings quality test.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Industry economics can dominate management effort
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Industry structure can overwhelm managerial effort, so weak returns must be diagnosed at the industry level before blaming management.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: A bad industry can neutralize talented management, while a favorable industry can make ordinary management look better; the investment decision must account for structural industry economics.
merge_decision_log.reason: Reinforces the recurring industry-structure-over-management principle.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: A fair-price good business beats a bargain bad business
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Business quality should outweigh mere cheapness because structurally weak businesses rarely become great investments through low price alone.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: Low price cannot reliably repair poor economics; weak businesses often require repeated fixes, absorb capital, and still fail to compound well, while good businesses can justify less dramatic entry prices.
merge_decision_log.reason: Adds the quality-over-cheapness and turnaround-trap principle.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Durable easy businesses can justify premium valuations
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Premium valuation can be justified when a business has durable, unusually favorable economics and high returns on tangible capital.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: When a business can earn very high returns on modest tangible capital for durable reasons, the value of future compounding can justify a price well above asset value.
merge_decision_log.reason: Adds a premium-valuation test within the quality-over-cheapness family.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Underwriting discipline matters more than premium volume
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: In insurance, underwriting discipline should take priority over growth in premium volume.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: Premium volume creates value only when priced profitably; chasing growth through underpricing destroys economics and often reveals weak underwriting culture.
merge_decision_log.reason: Reinforces underwriting discipline before premium volume.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Accept slack rather than write bad insurance
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Protect underwriting economics even if it means temporary underutilization or lower activity.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: Activity is not value creation; in underwriting, filling capacity with mispriced risk can destroy far more value than temporary slack costs.
merge_decision_log.reason: Adds the activity-versus-value capacity-utilization test under insurance volume discipline.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Insurance magnifies managerial talent and failure
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Insurance economics amplify the consequences of managerial judgment in pricing, reserving, and risk selection.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: Because insurance liabilities are delayed and leverage is embedded, small differences in pricing and reserving judgment compound into large economic differences.
merge_decision_log.reason: Extends people-driven advantage with insurance-specific judgment in pricing, reserving, and risk selection.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Long-tail insurance can hide bad economics
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Long-tailed liabilities can conceal poor underwriting economics for years, so current profitability must be treated cautiously.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: Delayed loss emergence allows poor underwriting to look good temporarily; the longer the tail, the greater the gap between reported results and economic reality.
merge_decision_log.reason: Sharpens reserve conservatism by emphasizing delayed long-tail loss emergence.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: High interest rates can worsen insurance cycles
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Investment income can distort underwriting incentives and make industry behavior collectively irrational in high-rate environments.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: When interest rates are high, insurers may tolerate underwriting losses to harvest float income, but this can normalize economically poor behavior across the industry.
merge_decision_log.reason: Adds the high-interest-rate float incentive distortion under float/underwriting quality.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Look through to retained earnings at investees
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Minority equity ownership should be evaluated using look-through earnings, not only reported accounting income.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: Value can compound inside holdings without appearing in reported earnings; the owner's true economics require looking through accounting to retained earnings at investees.
merge_decision_log.reason: Reinforces look-through earnings for retained earnings at investees.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Retained earnings are valuable only with strong allocators
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Retained earnings create value only when management can reinvest them at attractive incremental returns.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: Retention is not automatically good; value creation depends on whether management can deploy retained funds at attractive incremental rates and translate them into per-share gains.
merge_decision_log.reason: Reinforces retained earnings as valuable only when converted into per-share value through strong allocation.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Do not trade around excellent businesses for annual forecasts
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Portfolio changes should be driven by business quality and thesis changes, not by short-term market positioning.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: When the underlying businesses are strong and well-bought, long-term compounding matters more than aligning the portfolio with a one-year market view.
merge_decision_log.reason: Adds portfolio discipline against trading around excellent businesses for annual forecasts.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Avoid very long-term fixed-rate bonds in inflation
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Long-duration fixed nominal claims are unattractive when inflation threatens real purchasing power.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: A fixed claim on money becomes less valuable when currency purchasing power falls; the longer the duration, the more the owner is locked into poor real economics.
merge_decision_log.reason: Introduces fixed-income duration risk under inflation as a reusable principle.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Accounting can hide economic bond risk
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Accounting treatment should not substitute for economic reality when evaluating fixed-income risk.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: When accounting does not force recognition of market value changes, economic deterioration can persist unnoticed, leading decision-makers to underestimate risk.
merge_decision_log.reason: Introduces accounting-versus-economic reality specifically for fixed-income holdings.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Do not optimize around a structurally bad fixed-income setup
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Minor optimizations do not redeem an exposure whose underlying economics are structurally unattractive.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: When the underlying environment is hostile, tactical sophistication often only reduces pain rather than creating an attractive investment; avoidance may be superior.
merge_decision_log.reason: Adds a structural-exposure test: clever optimization cannot redeem poor fixed-income economics.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Optionality can shorten economic duration
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Meaningful optionality can reduce duration risk and improve the economics of fixed-income securities.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: Optionality can change a bond's economic profile by reducing time locked into a fixed claim and adding upside participation if conditions improve.
merge_decision_log.reason: Adds optionality as a duration-risk modifier within fixed-income analysis.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Low leverage plus high compounding is superior
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: High compounding is most valuable when it is achieved without stretching the balance sheet or operating structure.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: Compounding without leverage is more durable because it preserves resilience under stress and reduces the fragility of future returns.
merge_decision_log.reason: Adds durable low-leverage compounding as a distinct risk-and-quality principle.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Counterparty quality matters in business sales
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Business transactions should be judged partly by stewardship and counterparty quality, not only by headline price.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: Business ownership is a stewardship decision as well as a pricing decision; the long-term outcome for people, culture, and franchise quality can justify preferring a better counterparty over a slightly higher bid.
merge_decision_log.reason: Adds transaction stewardship and counterparty quality as a capital-allocation principle.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Outstanding businesses are hard to replace once sold
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Selling a high-quality compounding asset should be judged against the scarcity and replacement economics of what would be bought next.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: A high-quality business compounds internally in ways that cash cannot; once sold, comparable earning power may be unavailable or much more expensive to reacquire.
merge_decision_log.reason: Adds the replacement-economics test before selling scarce compounding assets.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Centralize capital allocation and decentralize operations
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Capital allocation and operations should be organized separately, with central financial discipline and decentralized operating autonomy.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: Operating excellence and capital allocation are different skills; combining central capital judgment with local operating autonomy can improve incentives, speed, and talent attraction.
merge_decision_log.reason: Adds the holding-company organization model: centralized capital allocation plus decentralized operations.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Great managers value autonomy like ownership
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Owner-like autonomy is a competitive advantage in recruiting and retaining high-agency managers.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: Talented operators prefer environments where judgment, responsibility, and freedom resemble ownership; organizational design can therefore become a source of managerial edge.
merge_decision_log.reason: Adds manager autonomy as a talent-attraction mechanism under organizational design.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Some mistakes are worth the benefits of decentralization
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: A zero-mistake organization may destroy more value through cost and bureaucracy than it saves through tighter control.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: Organizations trade off control against speed, cost, and talent attraction; accepting some mistakes can maximize long-run value when the overall system remains superior.
merge_decision_log.reason: Adds the decentralization trade-off: some mistakes can be cheaper than bureaucracy.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Report to owners like partners
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: Owner-oriented reporting is a governance signal that favors substance, candor, and stewardship over promotional optics.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: Candid reporting aligns managers with owners, improves governance, and helps build a shareholder base that understands the business in economic rather than promotional terms.
merge_decision_log.reason: Adds partner-like owner reporting as a shareholder-communication and governance principle.

merge_decision_log.incoming_title: Companies attract the shareholder base they deserve
merge_decision_log.semantic_fingerprint: A company's behavior and messaging recruit either long-term owners or short-term traders.
merge_decision_log.buffett_causal_logic: Management signals shape owner expectations; companies emphasizing short-term market metrics tend to attract transient holders, while economically grounded communication draws longer-term owners.
merge_decision_log.reason: Adds shareholder-base formation as a communication-quality sub-principle.

regression_test.notes: Whole-business test and partner-like reporting preserve owner orientation.
Framework captures durable economics, capital-light growth, cash returns on capital, and premium valuation for exceptional franchises.

regression_test.core_buffett_concept: High return on equity with little debt
regression_test.notes: 1979 adds explicit checks for leverage, gimmicks, and low-leverage compounding.
Cash earnings, working-capital absorption, and look-through earnings are present; the later formal owner-earnings formula is not yet fully supported by these source years.
Capital-light growth and high returns on tangible capital imply economic goodwill, but a fully explicit goodwill doctrine has not yet been developed in these processed years.
Allocation across uses, retained earnings, and sale/replacement economics are covered.
Retained earnings must create attractive incremental per-share value.
Price discipline and attractive-price filtering exist; the explicit margin-of-safety doctrine is not yet fully articulated by source years.
Understandable business is part of the four-part test, but the formal circle-of-competence concept is not yet separately developed.
Integrity, candor, owner orientation, and rational allocation are explicit.
Commodity economics, capital intensity, and hostile industry structure are preserved.

regression_test.core_buffett_concept: Float as a source of low-cost funding
regression_test.notes: Float is tied to underwriting quality and interest-rate incentive distortions.
Minority holdings and retained earnings at investees are covered.
Framework emphasizes long-term earning power, low-leverage compounding, and not selling rare compounding assets.
Economic reality over accounting form is preserved and extended to bond accounting.
High returns must be screened for leverage and durable compounding should not rely on balance-sheet stretching.

regression_test.core_buffett_concept: Share repurchases only below intrinsic value
regression_test.notes: Repurchases are present as an alternative use of capital and must be value-accretive, but a dedicated repurchase doctrine is not yet supported by the processed years.
1979 explicitly adds quality-over-cheapness and resistance to annual market forecasts.

regression_test.core_buffett_concept: Cost of growth / incremental capital needs
regression_test.notes: Growth is judged against incremental capital, cash conversion, and retained earnings productivity.
Framework includes accounting reconstruction, high-ROE validation, conservative underwriting reserve analysis, and bond accounting adjustment.

justified_overlaps_remaining.justification: All relate to returns, but they answer different analyst questions: capital required for earnings, whether high returns are distorted by leverage/accounting, and whether compounding is durable without leverage.
All relate to insurance quality, but they separately test float cost, volume discipline, and reserve/long-tail conservatism.
Parent tests quality over cheapness; sub-principle tests when premium valuation is justified by extraordinary durable economics.

metric_deduplication_notes: Normalized beginning_shareholders_equity to beginning_shareholder_equity where merged into the master.
Kept fixed-income risk separate from general accounting quality to improve retrieval for bond-duration and bond-accounting queries.
Used sub-principles for 1979 items that were related but analytically distinct, preventing parent principles from becoming kitchen sinks.
source_lineage_concerns: Source chunk IDs and source excerpts were unavailable in the provided JSON inputs, so they are set to null rather than invented.
Source file paths are preserved exactly as supplied by the extracted 1979 JSON.
schema_consistency_notes: Added primary_analytical_question, retrieval_aliases, decision_use_cases, agent_usefulness, regression_test, and post_merge_quality_audit fields.
Preserved existing source lineage and merge history while adding 1979 decisions.
agent_usability_notes: New categories real_owner_returns, owner_earnings_cash_generation, fixed_income_risk, risk_and_leverage, organizational_design, portfolio_management, and shareholder_communication improve retrieval specificity for future AI-agent analysis.
Each top-level principle now has a primary analytical question and retrieval aliases.
Sub-principles preserve distinct 1979 analytical tests without duplicating parent principles.
